stories.headline: Apollo Global Management acquires UK infrastructure platform in £4.2bn take-private amid PE dealmaking resurgence
stories.summary: Apollo Global Management has agreed to acquire a major UK-listed infrastructure investment platform in a deal valuing the target at approximately £4.2bn, marking one of the largest UK take-privates since the rate environment stabilised. The bidding consortium includes a sovereign wealth co-investor, with completion expected by Q3 subject to FCA and CMA clearance. Advisers on the deal include A&O Shearman (Apollo) and Freshfields (target board), with Lazard and Goldman running the M&A process. The premium represents a 28% uplift to the three-month average share price.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer, acting for the target board, is driving the Rule 9 offer document, the board independence process, and the complex CMA pre-notification filing given the infrastructure sector overlap. A&O Shearman, advising Apollo, brings the deepest UK PE advisory relationship in this asset class and will be running parallel FCA change-of-control and NSI Act national security review tracks simultaneously — a scope of work that practically excludes smaller teams. The CMA's heightened scrutiny of infrastructure acquisitions, particularly post-Rosebank, means both firms face an intensive regulatory advisory phase before any substantive completion mechanics.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Kirkland & Ellis will likely be advising Apollo on the fund structuring and LP capital calls triggered by this transaction, given their dominance of UK PE fund formation mandates — Apollo's European fund structures have historically been Kirkland work. For the leveraged acquisition financing, Latham & Watkins has consistently led on Apollo's European credit facilities, meaning the significant leveraged finance work on this deal will sit with Latham rather than with a Magic Circle team, creating direct competition between the US firm's finance bench and A&O's banking practice.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Freshfields, a first-seat trainee would be running the document room, tracking Condition Satisfaction Notices across the FCA and CMA parallel tracks, and preparing board minutes for each shareholder resolution required at completion. At Kirkland, an NQ corporate associate would be drafting limited partner notices and managing the subscription documentation for Apollo's co-investment pool. UK take-private volume in 2025 reached £38bn — the highest since 2007 — driven by persistent public market discounts and improving credit availability.

stories.talkingPoint: The Apollo-infrastructure deal exemplifies how the tension between private capital's 2026 deployment pressure and elevated UK public market discounts is converting into a structural take-private wave — and why firms with integrated M&A, financing, and regulatory benches are winning the biggest mandates.

stories.headline: Revolut confirms £8bn London IPO plans for H2 2026, testing appetite for high-growth fintechs on the LSE
stories.summary: Revolut has formally engaged Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley as global coordinators for a proposed London listing that would value the company at approximately £8bn, representing a significant discount to its 2021 private round at $33bn. The fintech secured its UK banking licence in mid-2025 and the IPO would mark one of London's most significant tech listings in years. Management will undertake a formal pilot-fishing exercise with institutional investors in April ahead of a potential June pricing window. The float would be the largest on the LSE since ARM's dual-listing.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Linklaters and Clifford Chance are the natural leads for the issuer-side mandate — both firms advised on every major LSE tech listing in recent years and have the ECM partner bench to handle an £8bn float. The prospectus liability work, FCA sponsor obligations under the UK Listing Rules, and structuring of Revolut's dual-class share architecture (permitted since the 2024 reforms) are squarely in their wheelhouse. Slaughter and May's corporate advisory practice will likely be engaged by certain institutional anchor investors, as it typically acts for the major UK asset managers taking cornerstone positions in high-profile London IPOs.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Sullivan & Cromwell and Davis Polk have advised Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley respectively on the majority of their significant global IPO mandates and both firms will likely hold the underwriting banks' mandates here. If Revolut pursues a Rule 144A private placement alongside the public float — likely given its US institutional investor base — substantial US securities law advisory work will sit firmly with the Wall Street firms, creating a dual-track structure where UK firms run the FCA process and US firms run the SEC registration.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Clifford Chance, a trainee would spend weeks on the verification exercise — cross-checking every factual statement in the prospectus against source documents, a painstaking but critical process for managing prospectus liability under section 90 of FSMA 2000. At Sullivan & Cromwell, a junior associate would be drafting the underwriting agreement and negotiating indemnity provisions with the Goldman and Morgan Stanley teams. London's share of global IPO proceeds has declined from 8% in 2019 to 4% in 2024 — Revolut's float is a live test of whether the FCA's Listing Rules reforms have reversed that structural drift.

stories.talkingPoint: Revolut's IPO will be the real-world verdict on whether the FCA's Edinburgh Reforms have made London genuinely competitive again for high-growth tech — or whether the discount to its last private round signals that structural confidence in the UK market hasn't fully recovered.

stories.headline: EU AI Act enforcement enters Phase 2 as first prohibited-use decisions land, exposing US tech firms to €35m fines
stories.summary: The EU AI Office issued its first binding decisions under Phase 2 of the AI Act, finding that two US-headquartered AI systems deployed in EU recruitment processes constituted prohibited biometric categorisation under Article 5. The decisions carry maximum fines of €35m or 7% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher. Both firms have 30 days to comply or appeal to the General Court of the EU. Phase 2 enforcement covers all 'high-risk' AI system categories including employment, credit scoring, and critical infrastructure management.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Clifford Chance's technology, media and telecommunications practice — with its Brussels and Frankfurt offices deeply embedded in EU regulatory process — is best positioned to advise US tech firms on their AI Act compliance posture and to manage enforcement proceedings before the EU AI Office. Linklaters' cross-border regulatory capability positions it for multi-jurisdictional mandates covering both AI Act and GDPR intersection work, which is becoming one of the fastest-growing areas of tech regulatory advisory. Herbert Smith Freehills, with its strong contentious regulatory practice, would be the natural choice for the General Court appeal if either firm mounts a legal challenge to the AI Office's decisions.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Cleary Gottlieb, Skadden Arps, and Davis Polk — all with established Brussels practices — are the natural counterparts to Magic Circle firms for US tech companies that want to run EU regulatory risk with US-trained partners who understand Silicon Valley risk tolerance. For active enforcement investigations rather than transactional compliance work, Covington & Burling and Gibson Dunn are significant presences in Brussels, frequently instructed by US tech majors facing EU regulatory action.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Clifford Chance, a trainee in the TMT regulatory team would be building AI system registers for clients, mapping each AI use case against the high-risk categories in Annex III of the Act, and drafting conformity assessment procedures for each system requiring certification. At a US firm's Brussels office, a junior associate would be preparing written submissions to the AI Office and researching the interaction between Article 5 prohibited uses and existing data protection impact assessments under GDPR Article 35. The EU AI Act compliance market is projected to generate €2.5bn in advisory fees across European law firms by 2027.

stories.talkingPoint: The EU's first AI Act enforcement decisions confirm that the 'grace period' for prohibited-use AI systems is over — and the 7%-of-global-turnover fine structure means that for a firm like Google or Meta, a single adverse decision could trigger a fine larger than most M&A deal values.

stories.headline: BP accelerates North Sea divestment programme with £1.4bn asset sale to Harbour Energy as transition strategy pivots
stories.summary: BP has agreed to sell a package of mature North Sea producing assets to Harbour Energy for £1.4bn in cash, with an additional £200m contingent on hydrocarbon price benchmarks through 2028. The disposal is part of BP's revised strategy — announced in February — to reduce upstream exposure and redirect capital to its gas and low-carbon transition portfolio. Closing is expected in Q4 2026 and requires NSTA approval. Linklaters is advising BP; Herbert Smith Freehills is acting for Harbour.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Herbert Smith Freehills, acting for Harbour Energy, has built the pre-eminent North Sea energy M&A practice over the past decade, advising on the majority of significant upstream asset transactions in the basin — this deal reinforces that positioning. Linklaters' energy practice has handled multiple BP upstream divestments and has an established relationship with the NSTA, the sector regulator whose approval is required for the licence transfer. The decommissioning liability mechanics — significantly more complex post-Rosebank following the Supreme Court's ruling on NSTA approval standards — create substantial additional advisory work around indemnity structuring, escrow arrangements, and long-stop decommissioning security.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Latham & Watkins' London energy practice is the primary US firm competitor for UK North Sea mandates and would be the natural choice to advise on Harbour Energy's reserve-based lending facilities, which will need to be restructured around the new asset base. White & Case has been active in integrated energy portfolio M&A and could hold a mandate on BP's wider portfolio restructuring strategy, particularly if BP pursues further disposals in the US or Middle East.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Herbert Smith Freehills, a trainee would be tracking NSTA pre-approval conditions, maintaining the asset schedule across multiple licence transfers, and coordinating the environmental liability disclosure exercise with the technical team. At Linklaters, a first-seat associate would be reviewing the reserve-based lending facility documentation to identify change of control provisions that require lender consent before asset transfer completes. North Sea upstream M&A reached a record £12bn in 2025, driven by majors systematically exiting mature producing assets to fund low-carbon transition capital.

stories.talkingPoint: BP's North Sea sell-down is not just an asset disposal story — it's a live illustration of how energy majors are using M&A to externally finance their transition, and why every energy-sector acquisition now comes with a decommissioning liability tail that changes the risk calculus for buyers and their financiers.

stories.headline: London Court of International Arbitration publishes record caseload figures as English governing law clauses recover post-Brexit
stories.summary: The LCIA's annual caseload report for 2025 shows a record 467 new arbitrations registered, with total amounts in dispute exceeding $23bn — a 34% increase on the prior year. English law remains the governing law of choice in 64% of cases, rebounding from a post-Brexit dip. The report notes a significant increase in disputes arising from energy transition commitments, distressed debt, and post-M&A warranty and indemnity claims. Financial services disputes account for 31% of new registrations by value.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer and Herbert Smith Freehills dominate international arbitration by caseload and billings, with both firms regularly acting in LCIA proceedings exceeding $500m in disputed value — HSF's international arbitration group is consistently ranked first in the LCIA's own statistics. Slaughter and May's disputes practice is consistently retained by major UK financial institutions, which account for 31% of LCIA caseload by value, as primary arbitration counsel. The growing pipeline of energy transition disputes and W&I insurance claims — the fastest-growing categories in the LCIA data — aligns directly with Freshfields' energy arbitration strength and Herbert Smith's post-M&A warranty work.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan and Gibson Dunn are the US firms with the strongest LCIA arbitration practices in London, regularly instructed by US-headquartered clients who prefer US-trained litigators even in English law proceedings. Latham & Watkins and White & Case are also significant presences, particularly in energy transition disputes where they hold deep sector relationships with US oil majors and sovereign energy companies seeking LCIA arbitration for cross-border contractual disputes.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Herbert Smith Freehills, a first-seat trainee in the arbitration team would be preparing hearing bundles for LCIA proceedings — a document-intensive exercise involving chronological organisation, witness statement cross-referencing, and tribunal bundle formatting to LCIA procedural requirements. At Quinn Emanuel, a junior associate would be drafting Requests for Arbitration, marshalling the evidentiary record, and preparing the Statement of Claim that formally opens proceedings. London's share of global arbitration seats has risen from 19% to 24% since 2022, driven partly by the post-Brexit divergence of English common law from EU legal harmonisation.

stories.talkingPoint: The LCIA's record caseload underscores that London remains the global default for high-value commercial arbitration — not by inertia, but because the combination of English common law sophistication, judicial neutrality, and enforcement through the New York Convention is genuinely difficult to replicate elsewhere.

stories.headline: US Department of Justice opens formal review of Big Four audit firms' UK operations under revised foreign oversight framework
stories.summary: The DOJ's newly established Foreign Audit Oversight Division has formally notified the UK's Financial Reporting Council that it intends to conduct direct oversight inspections of KPMG and PwC's UK audit practices covering US-listed UK companies. The move follows the 2025 bilateral agreement that extended PCAOB-equivalent oversight rights to DOJ in response to Congressional pressure over Sino-British audit disputes. The FRC has 60 days to respond with a formal cooperation framework. The first inspections are expected in Q3 2026.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Slaughter and May's regulatory practice is best positioned to advise the FRC on its response framework to the DOJ, given its deep relationships with UK financial regulators and track record on cross-border oversight disputes — the 60-day response window creates an urgent mandate. Clifford Chance and Herbert Smith Freehills — both with significant Big Four audit firm relationships and strong US regulatory expertise — will be in active dialogue with KPMG and PwC UK senior leadership on their cooperation posture, privilege protections, and governance restructuring required before the first inspections begin.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Sullivan & Cromwell, which has historically advised the major US audit oversight bodies and the SEC on international regulatory cooperation frameworks, is the obvious choice for the Big Four firms' US law advisory work on navigating the DOJ inspection regime. Davis Polk's regulatory practice has deep DOJ relationships and would likely be engaged by at least one of the firms to advise on the cooperation framework and on maintaining attorney-client privilege over documents within the inspection scope.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Clifford Chance, a trainee in the financial regulation team would be researching the 2025 bilateral oversight agreement, mapping the extent of PCAOB-equivalent inspection rights, and preparing a jurisdictional comparison of how similar overseas inspection regimes have operated in Germany, France, and Japan. At Sullivan & Cromwell, a junior associate would be drafting the written response to the DOJ notification and advising on attorney-client privilege protections for documents within the inspection scope under both English and US federal law. This development sits within a broader trend: US extraterritorial regulatory reach into UK professional services has expanded in five separate domains since 2022.

stories.talkingPoint: The DOJ's move into UK audit oversight isn't primarily a Big Four story — it's a signal of how US extraterritorial regulatory reach is expanding into professional services more broadly, and every UK-headquartered firm with US-listed clients should be stress-testing their compliance posture against this trajectory.

sectorWatch.body: Private equity dealmaking is showing its clearest signs of structural recovery since rates peaked: LP distributions have improved, credit markets have reopened at workable margins, and the bid-ask gap that paralysed M&A in 2023–2024 is compressing. The watch this week is whether improved sentiment converts into completed transactions — the pipeline is strong but execution remains the bottleneck as complex regulatory processes (CMA, FCA, NSI Act) stretch timelines.

oneToFollow.story: CMA cloud computing investigation expected to move to Phase 2 this week
oneToFollow.why: A Phase 2 finding could impose structural remedies on Microsoft, Amazon, and Google, with direct implications for technology M&A valuations, data centre lease structures, and commercial contracts across every sector reliant on public cloud infrastructure. Competition and tech teams at every major firm should be monitoring the provisional findings closely.
